DISCOVERY, DEPT. 302, OCT-01-2020 HEARING HELD RE: DEFENDANT HOWARD & 6TH STREET FOOD MARKET INC.'S MOTION FOR AN ORDER COMPELLING PLAINTIFF TO: PROVIDE WRITTEN RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES SET THREE; SET ONE; WRITTEN RESPONSES TO REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION SET TWO AND PRODUCE DOCUMENT; TO DEEM MATTERS ADMITTED IN NP'S REQUEST FOR ADMISSIONS SET ONE AND TO PAY MONETARY SANCTIONS. THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING WITH THE MODIFICATION OF INCREASING THE AMOUNT OF SANCTIONS AWARDED. THE MOTION TO COMPEL IS GRANTED, AND SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF $1,685 ARE AWARDED. (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING.) THE COURT WILL ISSUE A FORMAL ORDER. JUDGE PRO TEM: JEFFREY WOHL; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED. (302/JPT)

calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Oct-27-2021 Vacated

MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Feb-26-2021 Continued To Nov-08-21 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice Of Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default
Off Calendar Jan-28-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT QAIS SHUHTRAH's Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-defendant Qais Shuhtrah's motion to vacate the default entered on May 4, 2020 is granted. Shuhtrah demonstrates that he lacked actual knowledge of this action in time to defend and is entitled to relief pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 473.5.

Section 473.5(a) provides: "The notice of motion shall be served and filed within a reasonable time, but in no event exceeding the earlier of: (i) two years after entry of a default judgment against him or her; or (ii) 180 days after service on him or her of a written notice that the default or default judgment has been entered." This motion is timely. Cross-complainant has yet to obtain a default judgment as the Court rejected his request for entry of default judgment on December 2, 2020. There is also sufficient evidence to show that Shuhtrah did not file his motion more than 180 days after receiving notice of the entry of the default. Shuhtrah's moving and reply declarations demonstrate that he had no knowledge of this action until November 10, 2020. (Shuhtrah Decl., pars. 11, 12, 15; Shuhtrah Reply Decl., par. 6.) The evidence also demonstrates that the entry of the default was not the result of Shuhtrah's inexcusable neglect since his business partner was not forwarding his mail to him. The Court analyzes this motion "with the aim of implementing the policy of liberally granting relief so that cases may be resolved on their merits." (Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 547.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

Off Calendar Jan-15-2020 Continued To Mar-17-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-28-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Christopher Dewys, Esq. of Danfoura Law Offices for Defendant.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested/Discussed. Defendant seeks clarification of the tentative ruling. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

DEFENDANT HOWARD & 6TH STREET FOOD MARKET INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.Defendant's demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the second cause of action for intentional interference and overruled as to the first cause of action for breach of contract.

The demurrer to the second cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. Defendant Howard & 6th Street Food Market, Inc. is a party to the underlying contract in the action; therefore, it is not a "stranger" to the contract. As the Court previously ruled, a contracting party cannot be held liable in tort for conspiracy to interfere with its own contract. "The tort duty not to interfere with the contract falls only on strangers-interlopers who have no legitimate interest in the scope or course of the contract's performance." (Applied Equipment Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 514.)

The demurrer to the first cause of action is overruled. First, although there is no contract attached to the complaint, Code of Civ. Proc. 430.10(g) provides a ground for demurrer only if it "cannot be ascertained from the pleading whether the contract is written, is oral, or is implied." It can be ascertained here that the action is based on a written contract. In multiple places in the complaint, Plaintiff refers to articles of the agreement, leading to a reasonable understanding that the agreement is written, and not oral or implied in conduct. "Per Article 12 of the Agreement, Merchant (i.e. Food Market) agreed . . . Per Article 6 (J) of the Agreement, Merchant (i.e. Food market) agrees? (Compl 29-30, 41.) A written contract need not be attached, but may be pleaded either word for word or generally according to its legal effect. (Miles v. Deutsche Bank National Trust Co. (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 394, 402 ["a plaintiff may plead the legal effect of the contract rather than its precise language."]; McKell v. Washington Mutual, Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1489 ["A written contract may be pleaded either by its terms-set out verbatim in the complaint or a copy of the contract attached to the complaint and incorporated therein by reference-or by its legal effect."].)
Second, Defendant does not provide any authority that a plaintiff must allege facts establishing legal capacity to sue. A demurrer only challenges defects on the face of the complaint and treats the demurrer as admitting as true facts pled. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) It does not appear from the face of the complaint that Plaintiff lacks the legal capacity to sue. Moreover, Bus. & Prof. Code 17918 "only prohibits maintenance of the suit; it does not bar commencement of the action." (Am. Alternative Energy Partners II v. Windridge (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 551, 562 [plaintiff, an entity operating under a fictitious business name, had capacity to sue when it filed its complaint despite its failure to file the requisite certificate of limited partnership].)

In addition, Plaintiff shall file an amended complaint deleting the second cause of action within 10 days.

Order is signed.(see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-29-2019 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ABDULRAHMAN KHALED' DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Defendant Abdulrahman Khaled's demurrer to Plaintiff Farid Sharif's complaint is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc., 430.10(e).) The parties' meet and confer process was sufficient under Code of Civil Procedure 430.41. Plaintiff's second cause of action for intentional interference with contractual relations fails because Defendant Khaled is the owner and manager of Howard & 6th Street Food Market, Inc. (Compl. 9.) Howard & 6th Street Food Market, Inc. is a party to the underlying contract; therefore, Defendant Khaled is not a "stranger" to the contract. A contracting party cannot be held liable in tort for conspiracy to interfere with its own contract. "The tort duty not to interfere with the contract falls only on strangers-interlopers who have no legitimate interest in the scope or course of the contract's performance." (Applied Equipment Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 514; Mintz v. Blue Cross of Calif. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1594, 1603 ["it is settled that 'corporate agents and employees acting for and on behalf of a corporation cannot be held liable for inducing a breach of the corporation's contract'"].)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Oct-29-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT HOWARD & 6TH STREET FOOD MARKET INC. A CALIFORNIA, ABDULRAHMAN KHALED'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT.

Defendant Howard & 6th Street Food Market's motion to set aside default is denied. Defendant seeks discretionary relief from default on grounds of "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." (Code Civ. Proc., 473(b).) "In order to qualify for relief under section 473, the moving party must act diligently in seeking relief." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 234.) If there is a substantial delay between discovery of the default and defendant's filing a motion for relief under 473(b), defendant must show a reasonable excuse for the delay. (Ludka v. Memory Magnetics Int'l (1972) 25 Cal.App.3d 316, 321.)

Plaintiff's requests for attorney's fees and other court costs and appearance fees are denied. (See Code Civ. Proc., 473(b) [attorney's fees section applies to whenever relief is granted based on an attorney's affidavit of fault.].) Delays of three months or more routinely result in denial of relief absent satisfactory explanation for the delay. (See Stafford v. Mach (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1184; see also Minick v. City of Petaluma (2016) 3 Cal.App.5th 15, 34 ["A delay is unreasonable as a matter of law only when it exceeds three months and there is no evidence to explain the delay."].

Here, Defendant's default was taken on May 22, 2019, yet it did not file this motion until September 26, 2019, more than four months later. Defendant's only explanation for its delay relates to the period before the default was taken, but it supplies no facts about when it learned of the default (the request for default reflects on its face that it was served on Defendant in May), or why it waited until September to retain counsel and file this motion. (Khaled Decl. 2 ["The Defendant's failure to respond and/or take action prior to May 22, 2019 was excusable neglect since the Defendant reasonably relied on co-defendant Mr. Mohsen's promise to indemnify and defend it in the action." (emphasis added)]; Danfoura Decl. 2 [Defendant retained counsel on September 11, 2019.) Accordingly, the motion must be denied. (See Stafford, 64 Cal.App.4th at 1184 ["We have not found any case where relief from default judgment has been deemed proper where there was an extended period of delay and little or no explanation"] [trial court abused its discretion in granting relief where defendant did not take steps within a reasonable time to set aside default and default judgment].) Plaintiff's requests for attorney's fees and costs are denied, as the Court is authorized to grant such relief only if it grants relief "based on an attorney's affidavit of fault." (Code Civ. Proc. 473(b).)

Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
